data.title: Exxon Mobil Remains Woefully Underestimated
data.description:
Back in May of this year, I lauded Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) for the prospect of strong growth that the company had been accomplishing. Overall, the picture for the company looked good, with profitability leading up to that point rising compared to the same time a year earlier. But more important than that, thanks largely in part to acquisition activities, specifically its purchase of Pioneer Natural Resources in a deal that cost shareholders $68 billion on an enterprise value basis, the future looked bright. Management had been forecasting significant production increases over the next several years. And with that, the company would capture around $6 billion in structural cost savings.

Despite these positives, the company has fallen short of the "Buy" rating that I assigned it. You see, whenever I rate a company a "Buy," I expect the stock to outperform the market for the foreseeable future. But since then, shares are up only 8.9%, while the S&P 500 is up 17.3%. Given the amount of time that has passed and this underperformance, I wanted to revisit the picture. After all, nobody is perfect, and I could be wrong in my assessment. But after looking at the most recent data that management has provided, and the promise that it has offered of better days ahead, Exxon Mobil is still an appealing candidate.

A Great Play On Future Growth

Instead of focusing on the past, I think that for this article, the best emphasis is on the future. And this is because management has been very detailed regarding what they see going on moving forward. For instance, on Dec. 9, management provided a multiyear outlook that lays out where they think the company is heading. One thing that is clear is that management expects to significantly boost earnings and cash flow. For context, back in 2024, Exxon Mobil generated net income of $33.68 billion.

They expect to grow that by $25 billion by the year 2030. That would take net profits up that year to an astounding $58.68 billion. But that's nothing. Management expects that operating cash flow will grow from $55.02 billion in 2024 to roughly $90.02 billion by the end of the decade. These increases in profitability also represent an upward revision compared to what management previously thought. In the past, they expected earnings to only grow by $20 billion. And they expected cash flow to grow by roughly $30 billion.

Management's optimism centers around two different themes that the company is pursuing. First and foremost would be cost savings. In the past, Exxon Mobil was, according to management's own description, a very siloed business. In the image above, you can see precisely what I mean. But by 2022, the company had transformed into three main sets of operations, all under one umbrella. These kinds of restructuring activities have paved the way for cost reductions. For instance, instead of having over 10 functional ERP systems that created inefficiencies, the company has a single enterprise-wide solution. It plans to make other changes as well as time goes on, including through investments in additional technologies. Ultimately, the company expects that, compared to 2019, it will capture $20 billion in structural cost savings. Their previous forecast called for this to be $18 billion.

Of course, it is worth noting that most of those cost savings have already been baked into the company's financial picture. That's where the other side of profit increase is expected to come from. This involves earnings growth through production growth. You see, right now, the total potential addressable markets that the company plays in will be worth an estimated $4 trillion by the year 2030. And with global population growth expected to continue, and with certain catalysts driving energy demand higher, the company expects that market size to double to $8 trillion by the year 2050. This is no longer an oil company. In fact, it hasn't been for a while now. Management has been gradually transitioning this into a business that touches on all aspects of the economy, including things like the medical space, agriculture, emissions reduction activities, electronics, construction, mobility, packaging, and more.

When it comes to the Upstream operations of the company, management expects to achieve around $14 billion of earnings growth from 2024 through 2030. While cost reductions will play a role here, overwhelmingly the increase will come from higher volumes and changes in product mix. Management expects, for instance, for overall production to climb from 4.3 Moebd to 5.5 Moebd. That's actually up from the previous expected end point of 5.4 Moebd. And on top of this, the expected unit earnings per barrel have been revised higher by $2. I know that doesn't sound like much. But at scale, it is massive.

Another big driver of this earnings growth will be its Product Solutions business. This is the part of the company that consists of its energy products, chemical products, and specialty products businesses. For context, energy products include fuels, aromatics, catalysts, and certain licensing activities. Chemical products include olefins, polyolefins, and the like. And specialty products include things like finished lubricants, base stocks, synthetics, elastomers, and resins. What these all have in common is that these are value-added petroleum products and petrochemicals. In essence, management takes the products of its Upstream operations and refines those products into more valuable end goods.

This part of the company's management expects to see at least $9 billion of earnings growth from 2024 through 2030. $4 billion of this will come from advantaged projects, with 60% de-risked from completed projects already. And according to management, over 40% of the earnings growth coming from these operations will involve high-value products that the company continues to increase its emphasis on. The goal here is not just to capture additional revenue. It's to capture high-margin revenue.

We can also expect changes when it comes to its Low Carbon Solutions business. The entire reason for this unit is to build out a pipeline of emission reduction opportunities involving carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, lower-emissions fuels, lithium for the global battery and electric vehicle markets, and more. Management hasn't really provided a lot of detail here. But we do know already that its carbon capture and storage business is already significant. And when you look at all of its new businesses that are included under its corporate segment, the company expects to eventually grow earnings to $13 billion by the year 2040.

Not surprisingly, none of this will come cheap. Management is making significant investments to achieve these outcomes. This year, for instance, including cash acquisitions, the firm is expected to spend $29 billion on capital initiatives. In 2026, we can expect between $27 billion and $29 billion. And for the 2027 through 2030 fiscal years, we can expect between $28 billion and $32 billion annually. An increasing portion of capital expenditures will go toward early-stage major projects and early-stage policy-dependent and new business activities. And management expects that around $20 billion of lower emissions investments will be made from this year through 2030.

Projecting out what the future of the company will look like is difficult even with all of this factored in. But the good news is that management has given us some things to work with. They expect, for instance, to continue around $20 billion of share buybacks annually through next year. And on top of this, they also have provided a range of how much total cash flow the company should generate from its operating activities for the 2026 through 2030 fiscal years. For instance, if Brent oil prices average $55 per barrel, we can expect around $385 billion of cumulative cash flow. Around $250 billion is what the company needs to cover cash capital expenditures and its existing dividend. So this would leave an excess of $135 billion for shareholders. If oil averages $65 per barrel, excess will be $145 billion. And if it averages $85 per barrel, we can expect $245 billion in excess.

In the near term, I actually expect prices to go closer to the $55 range. Potentially even lower. And that's because of growing U.S. production and weakening economic conditions. But over the long haul, I believe that oil belongs somewhere between $60 and $80 per barrel. So for the purpose of this analysis, I will assume that the $65 per barrel is what ultimately is realized. Following this approach, we can see in the table below how much in annual operating cash flow, capital expenditures, share buybacks, and how much will be left between dividends and extra cash, the company should have through the end of this decade.

To have everything aligned, I did have to assume that, at some point throughout this time, the company would sell off assets worth about $7.26 billion. That's relatively small in the grand scheme of things. And it would not be uncharacteristic of the company to unload non-core assets. But because there is timing uncertainty, I assumed that this would happen at the very end of the forecast. In the table below, you can also see what we would be looking at in terms of changes in share count, the implied market capitalization of the company based on its current trading multiple, the implied price per share of the business, and how much total cash would come to shareholders between dividends and extra cash sitting on the company's books. You can also see total implied upside and implied annual upside. On an annualized basis, we are looking at an upside of around 15% from where the stock is right now. Considering that the S&P 500 typically averages around 10% or 11% a year, you can understand my optimism.

Takeaway

The way I see things, absent a plunge in energy prices that keeps prices low for an extended period, Exxon Mobil makes for a compelling prospect. The company is making big changes, as it has in the past. And if management is right, the future looks bright. Even if the trading multiples of the company remain unchanged from where they are right now, we are looking at market-beating potential. But I suspect that if management does start to deliver in a big way on this guidance, we could see further upside from an expansion of its trading multiples. Given this, I have no problem maintaining my "Buy" rating on the stock.

data.title: Moderna: Why I Am Selling The 'Relief Rally'
data.description:
Introduction

Moderna, Inc.’s stock (MRNA) has rallied ~23% since my last Sell rating in August.

Although I acknowledged the long-term potential of its CMV and personalized cancer vaccines, the company was burning $2-3 billion per year despite cost reductions. Meanwhile, its COVID-19 vaccine franchise, which made “Moderna” a household name, was in decline. Finally, I believed that Moderna’s other main product, mRESVIA (RSV), was likely to take a back seat to much larger competitors such as GSK plc (GSK) and Pfizer (PFE).

Moderna’s recent rally has me wondering if it has any “legs.” The article that follows reveals that Moderna’s fundamentals have arguably worsened since August, with the most recent rally likely being attributed to improved liquidity and cost discipline.

Financial Health

In November, Moderna released its Q3 earnings report, which featured healthy beats on revenue ($1.02 billion beats by $150 million) and EPS (-$0.51 beats by $1.67). The revenue beat was thanks to Moderna’s COVID vaccine, which generated $971 million in Q3. Moderna’s RSV vaccine, mRESVIA, brought in a paltry $2 million in sales. For reference, Moderna’s peers, Pfizer and GSK, generated $279 million and £0.3 billion (roughly $400 million) in Q3, respectively, for their RSV vaccines.

Despite the Q3 beat, Moderna narrowed its full-year 2025 revenue guidance and lowered the top end of US revenue guidance. Management also explicitly stated that they do not see US vaccination rates returning to flat, which caps any “rebound” narrative. And, make no mistake about it, while cash burn has lessened, it's still significant. Per Seeking Alpha, cash from operations (a proxy for cash burn) was ($847 million) in Q3.

Moderna has been making honest efforts to extend its cash runway. Its year-end cash and investments projection was increased from $6 billion to $6.5 billion to $7 billion. In late November, Moderna secured $1.5 billion in non-dilutive financing and, again, increased its year-end cash and investment projection to between $7.1 billion and $7.6 billion. Moreover, the company announced that it would cut four pipeline programs, including congenital CMV (as well as HSV, VZV, and GSD1a), signaling further cost savings.

This combination of lower 2025 operating expense guidance, a higher 2025 year-end cash balance, and the $1.5 billion loan materially reduces near-term dilution pressure. I suspect that this is the reason for Moderna’s recent stock rally.

Recent Developments

While CMV falling can be good for the cash story, it is bad for Moderna's intrinsic pipeline value. Discontinuing CMV removes what was previously lauded as a potential billion-dollar class vaccine.

As I highlighted in my last Moderna write-up, CMV was a key near-term vaccine growth pillar, and something that could have made me optimistic on the name had the program succeeded. Without it, Moderna’s dependence on the remaining respiratory franchise and the Merck-partnered (MRK) oncology program increases.

Simultaneously, mNEXSPIKE share gains do not solve the core demand constraint. As the company noted in its Q3 earnings call, “cumulative retail vaccinations were 13.2 million, down approximately 30% year-over-year.” In essence, Moderna’s COVID franchise is fighting for a larger slice of a smaller pie. Eventually, this will catch up to it.

Critically, Moderna’s Q3 mNEXSPIKE performance requires some nuance. Management’s seasonal working capital explanation for its Q3 cash decline implies front-loaded channel fill dynamics. Basically, Q3 revenue is predominantly a feature of build, rather than actual demand, that must convert into “shots in the arm,” which usually happen during Q4 (vaccination season).

The company admitted they are deferring Phase 3 spend in infectious disease until after breakeven and are seeking partners for Phase 3 assets like EBV. Again, while this stretches its cash, it effectively says it cannot or will not self-fund large pivotal programs in the near term, increasing its dependence on partners.

Moderna’s own 2028 cash breakeven projection appears to hang more on cost savings than it does revenue acceleration. The only near-term revenue levers management was able to cite were contracted government partnerships and geography, not new product ramps. This implies that Moderna is prioritizing survival over growth visibility.

Final Words

To wrap up, Moderna’s recent stock rally looks like a “sigh of relief” from market participants. Moderna has effectively extended its cash runway and is no longer in imminent danger.

However, if you take a step back, the core, long-term problems are still there and have, arguably, deteriorated since my last look. The COVID vaccination market is in perpetual decline. Although mNEXSPIKE is making up for this in the interim by securing more market share, this cruel reality remains. Moreover, Moderna’s mRESVIA has to settle for the “back of the line” in a highly competitive RSV vaccine market. Once hoped to reinvigorate its infectious disease pipeline, congenital CMV has been "thrown in the trash" in an effort to boost Moderna's near-term financial outlook.

So, these “positive” financial developments must be viewed in a proper context. Moderna is essentially cannibalizing itself in an effort to be lighter and faster. However, I propose that this type of strategy is unlikely to win the long-distance races inherent in drug development.

Net-net, despite the apparent "relief rally," I am still cautious on Moderna's long-term prospects (maintain “Sell” and “market underperform”).

Some things that could change my mind include U.S. COVID vaccination stabilization, mRESVIA showing material traction, flu mRNA 1010 and flu plus COVID mRNA 1083 achieving approvals and demonstrating real demand, and a quantitatively supported bridge to 2028 cash breakeven that relies on revenue drivers instead of mere cost cuts.

data.title: Intel's Most Unusual Bull Case
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Intel Corporation’s (INTC) investment case is increasingly anchored in U.S. industrial policy rather than near-term fundamentals. Massive government backing, alongside strategic capital from partners like Nvidia (NVDA), de-risks its costly foundry pivot and shores up the balance sheet.

INTC has already rerated meaningfully since earlier coverages, yet the stock has largely stalled since my last coverage in October, trading in a holding pattern as the market waits for a clear, next catalyst to justify another leg higher.

While execution risks and foundry losses persist, Intel’s role as a national semiconductor champion reframes timelines, attracts patient capital, and improves the odds that today’s cash burn evolves into long-term strategic value.

U.S. Policy Backing Injects Billions, De-Risks Intel Foundry Pivot

The main bullish factor for Intel is its deepening integration with the United States' national industrial and security policy. It started as a matter of getting a government grant, and now Intel is repositioning as a crown jewel of American tech sovereignty.

This positioning brings in capital, de-risks an expensive business model transition, and creates a market theme that can attract more deals and government favor beyond near-term financial issues. So my analysis begins with the direct capital infusions that are reshaping Intel's balance sheet. There is accelerated funding from the United States government, and there are important investments from Nvidia and SoftBank Group (SFTBY).

In Q3 alone, Intel received $5.7 billion from the US government. Back in August, the Trump Administration invested $11.1 billion for a ~10% stake in Intel. This influx of capital makes Intel a top priority for the government through shoring up the balance sheet. Intel repaid $4.3 billion of debt in Q3. The government's direct financial backing directly addresses Intel’s point of financial vulnerability and provides Intel with the cash to pursue its capital-intensive foundry track without facing historical market pressures.

I believe this financial backing is a result of Intel’s central role in national actions. For me, the Genesis Mission is a big example. Intel is named as one of 24 companies partnering with the U.S. Department of Energy to accelerate discovery science, solidify national security, and energy advancements. The mission’s aim is to build the national AI platform for scientific discovery.

Thus, Intel is now a foundational partner in a national strategic endeavor. This positions Intel at the core of the U.S.'s response to global competition in AI, manufacturing, and energy. CEO Lip-Bu Tan confirmed this on the Q3 call.

I'm honored by the trust and confidence President Trump and Secretary Lutnick have placed in me. support highlights Intel's strategic role as the only US-based semiconductor company with leading-edge logic R&D and manufacturing.

Moreover, he stated, Intel is

fully committed to advancing the Trump administration's vision to restore semiconductor production.

Further, the current positioning of Intel supports Wall Street’s bullish tone for Intel's highly expensive project (the Intel Foundry). The semiconductor manufacturing is mostly concentrated at TSMC (TSM). There will be bad outcomes for the U.S. tech sector if China takes action against Taiwan. So, the chip production outside of Taiwan by Intel is essential for a diversified supply for the U.S. tech sector.

The U.S. government's investment and vocal backing are clearly targeting building a domestic, at-scale, leading-edge alternative to TSMC. Intel Foundry is that alternative. Lip-Bu Tan has a plan to "work with the U.S. government within the secure enclave." Overall, the entire foundry project is framed as a matter of national economic security, which makes its success a government priority.

Beyond policies, my analysis of Intel's Q3 and its outlook points to several bullish signals. The most immediate is the massive earnings beat in Q3. Intel reported revenue of $13.65 billion and a non-GAAP EPS of $0.23, beating the EPS consensus estimate by a massive magnitude of $0.01. The outcome shows that there would have been a much more superior performance.

I also see a much-improved financial position with $30.9 billion in cash after repaying $4.3 billion of debt. This reflects my point that injection of capital from the government and partner investments provides Intel a solid footing to fund its operational and CapEx plans.

For the outlook, the forecast for the PC market is also encouraging. The client consumption TAM may hit 290 million units in 2025, which is the fastest TAM growth since 2021. This points to a healthy demand for Intel's largest business segment. The new collaboration with Nvidia also opens a new avenue for growth by combining Intel’s CPUs with Nvidia's NVLink tech. It is positioning Intel to address new parts of the AI market (like AI PCs).

Foundry Burns $2.3 Billion Quarterly, Delaying Profitability Into 2026

As I review the financials and management's own commentary in the Q3 call, I see a big weakness still there for Intel. There is a disconnect between the strategic importance of the Intel Foundry and its current operations and financials. The entire turnaround story and the justification for its national champion status lie in the foundry’s business success.

Yet this segment is currently a large financial drain with clear execution challenges to its profitability. The most direct pattern of this problem is in the numbers, as Intel Foundry has an operating loss of $2.3 billion in Q3. This loss was on revenue of $4.2 billion for the segment.

An operating loss of this size points to the fact that the business is actively burning through large amounts of cash. This is sharply against the Intel products segment that generated $3.7 billion in operating profit. It means the new strategic direction of Intel is currently erasing a large portion of the profits from its legacy business. The gross profit margin of 38% improved YoY, but the foundry's high unprofitability works as a drag on the bottom line.

Further, the execution on manufacturing yields for Foundry’s new technologies is not yet at an economically viable level. The yields can address the supply, but they are not at the level to drive up the margins. As per the CFO, it may take a year. This is a direct admission that for at least the next year, the core tech of the foundry may not be profitable on a per-unit basis. This is an execution risk, as the entire financial model of a foundry depends on high-yielding and cost-effective manufacturing.

Intel is signaling that this is still a work in progress. As per the CFO, the forecast for a lower gross margin in Q4 of 36.5% is partly due to the impact of the first Core Ultra 3 shipments, which have a higher cost than in the early stages of product ramp.

As Intel tries to move its products to its own new advanced foundry nodes like 18A, I think it will continue to face margin dilution in the upcoming quarters. Why? These nodes are expensive and have lower yields initially. Intel is in a difficult position where its stock price upside depends on the market adoption of these new nodes, as slow adoption will hurt its short-to-mid-term profitability.

Finally, this problem is compounded by capacity limits on its older, more mature, and presumably more profitable manufacturing processes. Capacity constraints on Intel 10 and Intel 7 limited Intel from fully meeting demand in Q3 for both data center and client markets. This means that at the same time its new foundry processes are unprofitable and dilutive, Intel cannot satisfy demand for its older, more cost-effective chips.

Takeaway

Intel is no longer just a turnaround bet; it’s a policy-backed strategic asset. Government capital lowers financial risk, extends the runway, and shifts investor focus from quarterly pain to long-term relevance. Execution still matters, but national priority status meaningfully tilts the risk-reward balance in Intel’s favor.

data.title: MORT: An Overview Of This U.S. Mortgage REIT ETF
data.description:
This article covers the VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF (MORT) as part of our educational series, initiated by Seeking Alpha. It's interesting times for mortgage investors, given the departure from the pre-COVID, low-rate, low inflation environment. Therefore, I deem it an excellent time to explains the ins and outs of a vehicle such as MORT ETF.

Without further ado, here's everything you need to know about this mortgage REIT ETF.

Fund Facts

The ETF is a tracker fund, benchmarked to the MVIS® US Mortgage REITs Index (MVMORTTG), which represents the performance of U.S.-listed mREITs as a whole. Said differently, MORT provides investors with diversified exposure to U.S. mREITs, isolating systematic risk and return dynamics. The vehicle currently charges its investors an expense ratio of 0.42% and provides a quarterly distribution (discussed later in the article).

The ETF is U.S. and mostly residential-centric. However, peripheral exposure to commercial mortgages is evident via mREITs such as Blackstone Mortgage Trust, Inc. (BXMT) and Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance, Inc. (ARI). Minimal foreign exposure is displayed; this vehicle is primarily influenced by U.S. mortgage markets. In addition, names such as RITHM Capital Corp (RITM) have diversified revenue sources, with servicing rights being noteworthy.

A look at MORT's historical returns (figure 3) illustrates investors' dependence on distributions. The ETF's price decay has been notable, largely driven by return of capital and COVID-era mREIT devaluations. In addition, cyclical performance is evident.

Side note: As of December 22nd, 2025, MORT had a 30-day SEC yield of 12.60%.

As previously mentioned, MORT distributes quarterly and delivers return of capital. ROC isn't taxed directly, but it reduces an investors' cost basis, which can provide tax implications, indirectly — here is a link elaborating on ROC's tax implications.

Key Drivers

Duration

In an oversimplified world, interest rate duration pays reference to the sensitivity of a fixed income product's price relative to a parallel shift in an interest rate curve. In theory, bonds with positive duration will gain value when interest rates fall and vice-versa. Mortgages mostly act the same, though academics will often suggest mortgages have negative duration (due to prepayments being higher when rates fall, which erodes value). However, in reality, it's only when severe rate shocks occur, that we would see mortgages reach negative duration, figure 5 provides a COVID-era case study of the negative duration phenomenon.

The following diagram illustrates the inverse relationship quite well. However, note two additional factors. Firstly, option-adjusted spreads (which resemble credit risk) also play a role, and secondly, the ETF has an income component; the income component and the price component can show inverse relationships.

So, based on duration, how would I trade or invest in this ETF?

I would wait for the risk premium to open up. What I mean by that, is I would wait for interest rates to peak, which would've created a scenario as follows.

  1. High interest rates diminished MBS prices. Subsequent declines in interest rates would likely result in price gains.
  2. Coupons on newly-issued and secondary market MBS have risen, contingent on higher interest and mortgage rates. Subsequent declines in rates would see coupons narrow, yet at a lagged interval, where repricing gains occur long before coupons lower (the portfolio would first need to be re-invested into a material amount of new issues for coupons to decline).

Collectively, I'd hope to profit from a duration affect from both interest and mortgage rates, accompanied by a lagged decrease in coupons.

Prepayments and Servicing Rights

Despite its impact being over-estimated at times, the conditional prepayment rate does impact valuations, especially among liquid MBS products. The U.S. Federal Housing Financing Agency provides useful updates on CPR. Be aware that CPR isn't just influenced by rates, but also seasonality, mortgage age, and economic conditions. Generally, a very low-rate environment with low weighted average lives on mortgages, supported by a strong economy, would result in higher CPR and lower MBS values.

Furthermore, some of MORT ETF's holdings generate substantial revenue from servicing rights; RITHM Capital provides an example. Servicing revenues would rely on volume. The best way to keep track of volume would be to look at new originations (figure 7). Alternatively, a view of MORT's largest holdings' investors presentations, would provide value.

Commercial Mortgages' Reliance on Fundamentals

By market cap, about 30% of U.S. Mortgage REITs span commercial mortgages. Considering MORT ETF's efforts to track broader U.S. REIT exposure, we can likely experience similar throughout-the-cycle exposure. Despite commercial mortgages' smaller representation and their shared traits (with residential mortgages), we need to acknowledge MORT ETF's exposure to fundamental factors that might influence its total returns.

Firstly, visiting issuer-specific investor presentations might provide an idea of the ETF's sector exposure. As an example, I embedded figure 8, which shows Blackstone Mortgage Trust's exposure; a discussion of key markets worth following is discussed post-figure 8.

Here are a few fundamental markers worth following.

  • Option-Adjusted Spreads: Option-adjusted spreads are market-based variables, that illustrates the market's perception of credit risk. Wider spreads would signal the market's growing concerns of issuers' abilities to repay debt.
  • Sector Debt Coverage Ratios: Industry or sector-specific debt coverage will tell us a lot about counter-part risk. Ratios such as interest coverage, debt-service coverage, debt levels, and EBITDA are generally considered key markets. Keep in mind, the ratios would be somewhat contingent on economic health, meaning, general macro indicators might provide value.
  • Asset Class-specific Regimes: Regime-dependence is critical to performance. For example, the work-from-home phenomenon crushed office properties during COVID-19. Another example of regime-dependence is data centers, amid a surge in cloud computing and AI.

The Underlying Issuers Present Divergent Performance

A noteworthy feature of MORT ETF is the divergent performance of its underlying holdings, illustrated by figure 9. Although its holdings share similar systematic traits, company-specific management and investor trust have proven influential. I cite a simple example, the ETF has exposure to Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX) and ARMOUR Residential REIT, Inc.(ARR), which operate very similar structures in the levered RMBS space. However, ARMOUR Residential has underperformed Dynex by over 90% in the past 10-years.

In my view, key differentiators among underlying holdings will be leverage, asset timing, hedging, and management's abilities to cope with tail risk.

Valuations and Market Risk Appetite

In figure 10, I highlight some of the underlying index's fundamental valuation metrics, price performance, and an annual volatility indicator. Tracking price-to-book is important for an mREIT, as is its dividend yield. In addition, the volatility line-item provides color to investors' risk-return regime. If volatility gradually starts increasing, we are likely headed for a more risk-averse market, which could be disadvantageous to MORT ETF. Generally speaking, we'd like to see yields high, price-to-book below 1x, and volatility consistent.

Portfolio Suitability

Timing

MORT ETF is a macro factor timer's dream. The ETF reacts to interest rates, mortgage rates, credit spreads, and the real economy – all cyclical variables. The problem with macro factor timing isn't its complexity, but the time requirements to track the macro factors and act on them, making it impractical for most retail investors.

Income-Seeking

A more heuristic use for MORT ETF would be to use it as a income engine within a broadly diversified portfolio, illustrated by the fact that its current 30-day SEC yield is ~840 basis points higher than the U.S. 10-year yield. Historical capital losses raise concerns. However, realized losses can be used to offset outsized capital gains.

Risk Factors

The COVID-19 pandemic reminded us how the mortgage market can provide a false sense of security, when MORT fell by nearly 70%. Although MORT might trade within acceptable risk bounds during normal market events, it has shown to be susceptible to crash risk, when a black swan event surfaces.

Furthermore, mortgages can chronically underperform during certain regimes. Their dependence on steady rates, moderate inflation, and solid demand creates a situation, whereby investors need to think about the economic regime, as opposed to relying on throughout-the-cycle performance.

Lastly, as mentioned before, some of MORT ETF's underlying holdings operate on high leverage. Leverage wrecks balance sheets when applied incorrectly. Moreover, leverage is interconnected, a mass credit event can ripple throughout mREITs, leaving MORT ETF with systematic headwinds.

Conclusion

The VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF reflects the performance of the broader U.S. mREIT environment by tracking an underlying index. The vehicle has shown cyclical performance throughout its tenure, largely driven by rate regimes. Moreover, MORT ETF's holdings have delivered divergent performance, revealing the make-or-break landscape of mortgages, and leveraged mortgages specifically.

Furthermore, MORT ETF delivers a high distribution yield and possesses an expense ratio of around 0.42%. However, care should be given to the fact that the ETF occasionally returns capital, which has led to some NAV erosion over time.

In closing, MORT ETF can play a role in market timing portfolios and as an income-engine for diversified portfolios. That being said, careful understanding of the ETF's key drivers is essential as MORT ETF is more complex than it looks at face value.

This article answers three main questions about MORT:

  1. What kind of holdings does MORT have?
  2. What influences MORT's returns and yield?
  3. How can MORT fit into a portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: It's Christmas Already For Metals - Gold, Silver And Platinum Trading Levels
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Christmas trading can be both uneventful and chaotic - some traders rush to exit their long-held positions to take a stress-free holiday rest.

The absence of counteracting parties leads to more erratic flows, as seen in this morning’s stock market action.

But away from the traditional Christmas stock market trading, metals trade around global exchanges on a different set of fundamentals.

And it seems that they are the most beneficent victims of Santa's flow.

Just today, gold came very close to $4,500 before retracting somewhat, but allowed silver, platinum, and palladium to reach multi-year highs (or set new records in the case of silver).

Ranging from 0.70% for the yellow metals to above 5.50% for platinum and palladium, they are grabbing all the attention of the few traders that still have skin in the game before 2025 ends.

It could be the final bouts of volatility for markets, but keep a close eye on whether geopolitical tensions arise, which could add more fuel to commodities.

As many institutions are out to defend certain products, you can also attempt to capture some rare holiday algorithmic patterns - these require thorough study of charts and may not be found until a while.

Let's dive in for a quick overview of the daily flows and some short-term trading levels for gold (XAUUSD:CUR), silver (XAGUSD:CUR) and platinum (XPTUSD:CUR).

Gold 4H Chart and Technical Levels

Levels to watch for gold (XAUUSD:CUR) trading:

Resistance levels

  • $4,497 current all-time high
  • Potential new ATH resistance (measured move) $4,500-4,575
  • 1.618% Fibonacci projection $4,687

Support levels

  • Preceding all-time high pivot $4,300-4,400
  • Key support and triangle top $4,200-4,240
  • 50-day MA $4,150
  • Major pivot $3,950 to $4,000 (200-period MA)
  • $3,700 consolidation support
  • $3,500 major support

Platinum 4H Chart and Technical Levels

Up 10% in the past 24 hours and not stopping anywhere, it really could attempt a move to catch up to gold if things continue that way (even if there's quite some way to go towards that target).

Platinum technical levels to keep on your charts:

Resistance levels

  • $2,299 March 2008 all-time high
  • Session highs $2,275
  • $2,500 psychological level

Support levels

  • May 2008 support $2,050-2,100
  • $1,950 past day highs retest support
  • 2025 channel upper bound $1,850 (mini-support)
  • 2013 and current-year highs $1,700-1,750
  • $1,620 to $1,650 FOMC support
  • Major high time frame pivot $1,500-1,600

Silver 4H Chart and Technical Levels

A potential Fibonacci resistance is coming up at around $71.40 at a confluence with the top of the 4H channel. Above this, there will be no resistance until the $75 psychological level.

Levels to watch for silver (XAGUSD:CUR) trading:

Resistance levels

  • Daily highs and potential resistance $70-71.40
  • $71.10 session highs
  • $75 potential psychological resistance

Support levels

  • Pivot $65-67 at previous all-time highs
  • Major intraday support $61-63
  • Pre-FOMC support $58.00-60

Safe trades, and Merry Christmas!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: KBE: Best-In-Class Structure, But Cyclical Risk Comes With The Territory
data.description:
Even though the State Street SPDR S&P Bank ETF (KBE) was born as a solution to represent the US banking segment, the nature of the reference index leads it to always maintain a certain imbalance toward the regional banking segment. Which gives KBE a “softened” small cap factor that makes the ETF very fascinating and certainly distinctive compared to more widely used solutions like XLF or KRE. But in what way is KBE built, what kind of exposure does it bring in a portfolio? and who is it aimed at? All very valid questions. But let’s go step by step.

Introduction to the State Street SPDR S&P Bank ETF

KBE is an ETF managed by SSGA Funds Management, Inc. in a completely systematic manner and without discretionary intervention, launched on November 8, 2005. With an AUM of ~$1.4 bn, the fund replicates the total return of the S&P Banks Select Industry index, which represents the US banking segment (money center + regional banks). The method used by KBE is Modified Equal Weight, which is adjusted through quarterly rebalancing.

The expense ratio is 0.35%, to which a negligible bid/ask spread of 0.02% is added, not to mention a low turnover (22%). Overall, however, this is covered by a dividend yield of 2.60%.

What Does KBE Do?

It makes sense to start from the index inclusion criteria: this selects solutions with a float-adjusted market cap over $2B and a liquidity coverage ratios over 100%. First consideration: even though it selects around 100 components, the index is effectively drawing from a very large pool of stocks. At this point, if an equal weight inclusion method is also added, it is natural that there will be direct impacts on asset allocation: this in fact reduces the dominance of money center banks and amplifies the weight of regional banks.

In fact, with over 74% in regional banks, we can say that KBE is a solution primarily oriented toward portfolio coverage of the US regional banking segment rather than a global financial exposure as one might have erroneously inferred from the name. This makes KBE highly sensitive to the risk and return drivers of the regional banking segment, and especially small cap.

Who Is KBE For?

As a result, by exclusion, KBE will not be suitable for those looking for a diversified, global exposure to the financials segment. Rather, it is aimed at those seeking a US-centric solution, skewed toward the regional banking segment. And this has distinctive consequences in portfolio terms. First of all, on momentum:

The expansion and contraction cycles of the regional banking segment more closely follow the dynamics of the small cap value market, rather than the S&P 500. In this sense, the parallel with my pick from a small cap perspective, AVUV, is interesting.

Likewise, they share the same risk, therefore volatility. In fact, the volatility of KBE, even though financials are often associated with a defensive sector, is higher than that of the S&P 500. Precisely because its nature leads it to be more comparable to broader benchmarks such as the Russell 2000.

This makes it a solution poorly suited for those seeking defensive assets: an annualized volatility of 25% brings portfolio volatility, certainly not protection.

For this reason, KBE could be proposed as a tactical or satellite portfolio solution rather than a core holding or a full representation of the financials segment component within a diversified portfolio.

How Is KBE Built?

103 well-distributed stocks (only 11.46% in the top 10) with an average market cap of ~$30 bn, which confirms the real mid-cap bias typical of the regional banking segment.

At this point, the confirmation lies precisely in the sector distribution, which can be outlined as follows, confirming over 74% in regional banks, and just over 8% in commercial and residential mortgage finance.

The equal weight nature emerges clearly here: stocks such as JPM, Citi, Wells are performance stabilizers that are practically irrelevant because they weigh the same as a regional bank.

And we also find confirmation in valuations: the forward P/E is low, at 11.88x; the reason should be sought in the estimated EPS growth, here equal to 15.04%, with a P/B. These are valuations clearly lower at first glance than both the S&P 500 and more widely recognized ETFs such as XLF (respectively with P/E of 27x and 18x). At the same time, it is necessary to pay attention to a series of elements:

  • The expected EPS is very cyclical, it assumes credit stability, no recession, and normalization of funding costs.
  • Relative valuations have a historical discount, but one that is consistent with a highly cyclical sector such as small cap value, and not comparable in absolute terms with the S&P 500 and XLF.

Risks

On the one hand, the equal weight method brings diversification; on the other, at least in the financial sector, it also leads to a neutralization of quality. This is because it results in a flattening of the difference between a solid balance sheet and a fragile one, which makes the ETF more sensitive to credit dynamics from an overall perspective.

A structure composed of over 80% Regional Banks and Mortgage Finance entails high sensitivity to macro drivers that are often difficult to control and/or predict, such as real rates, the yield curve, commercial real estate trends, and credit quality. And today, with credit conditions remaining fairly tight, with open adjusted spreads at multi-year lows, this is not an element to forget.

Peer comparison

At this point, naturally, the peer table should be oriented toward solutions exposed at least predominantly to the regional banking sector. In this sense, here is a fairly exhaustive peer table of the segment.

Simply extending performance to 5Y, an outperformance of KBE can be observed, even though, looking a bit further back, it is clear that this has not always been the case. During the 2022 dump, with the tightening of credit conditions, KBE was also one of the worst performers. This confirms its relatively volatile nature and sensitivity to monetary policy expectations. It should not be forgotten that, albeit in a limited and marginal way, KBE also includes solutions that are extraneous to the regional bank segment, an element that, for example, distinguishes it from other more niche solutions such as KRE. This is why returns and volatility often diverge.

That said, it still remains the solution with the most advantageous expense ratio together with KRE and KBWR, and the one with the highest AUM among the selected peers together with KRE.

Pros & Cons:

KBE has distinctive elements that work in its favor, which can be summarized as follows:

  1. An equal-weight structure, which entails better management of idiosyncratic risk. Here, all stocks account for ~1–1.35%.
  2. Compressed valuation compared to US equity and broad financials, which could signal a negative asymmetry that is already partially priced in.
  3. A competitive expense-to-distribution ratio, especially thanks to the presence of a positive dividend yield.

And naturally, KBE is not without flaws:

  1. Strong factor concentration in regional banks
  2. Which gives it negative sensitivity to the rate-cutting cycle
  3. And the absence of internal stabilizers within the portfolio: stocks such as JPM, Citi, and Wells Fargo weigh the same as a regional bank and do not provide an anti-cyclical function.

This article answers these three main questions about KBE:

  • Does KBE have international holdings or is it strictly domestic?
  • Is KBE subjected to cyclical risks as a result of investing only in the banking sector?
  • What type of investor is KBE best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Nvidia: 2026 Is Likely To Be A Year Of Consolidation (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Nvidia Faces A Mixed Set Of Catalysts

I last covered Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) on Nov 13 with an article titled "Nvidia Q3 Earnings Preview: Robust Margins Met With A China Problem.” That article served as a preview of its Q3 earnings report (ER, released on Nov 19) and rated the stock as a Hold. Since then, a mixed set of new catalysts have surfaced around this leading chipmaker.

In the rest of this article, I elaborate my thoughts on 3 developments since the ER: the new development with its China market, the rise of custom silicon, and also the latest technical trading patterns. These developments have led me to the year of 2026 as a year of consolidation for the stock. As the AI sector continues to transition from its initial explosive growth phase into a period of infrastructure maturity in 2026, Nvidia faces a mixed set of challenges and opportunities due to these developments from uncertainties of global trade and intensified competition from hyperscalers.

Let me start with the development of its China market. The latest development on this front keeps presenting a complex outlook for Nvidia in 2026. For readers unfamiliar with the background, President Trump approved the export of Nvidia's H200 GPUs to China earlier this month. More specifics are quoted below:

Seeking Alpha News: Nvidia (NVDA) has plans in place to start shipping its H200 GPUs to China by the middle of February, assuming government approval… Assuming government approval, Nvidia will add production capacity, with orders coming from the added production in the second quarter. President Trump said earlier this month that he had approved the sale of Nvidia's H200 GPUs, which are less powerful than its Blackwell line, to China, for a 25% fee.

This development seems positive at first glance. However, after thinking about it more, I am not certain A) if it is all positive, and B) even if it is, how much impact it eventually would have on its bottom line. To start, based on its Q3 financials (see the pie chart below), NVDA’s China sales only account for a relatively small portion of its total revenue (about 13%). I expect the impact on the bottom line to be even smaller due to the 25% fee and other considerations. The H200 GPUs are less advanced and powerful than its Blackwell line, and I thus expected lower margins on these exports. Furthermore, the demand in China is uncertainty too in my view. As detailed in my last article, Chinese domestic AI chips are advancing at a good pace, and many end users there might be reluctant (or discouraged by the central government) to use NVDA chips.

To further compound the uncertainty, internal chip development by major U.S. customers like Alphabet/Google (GOOGL) is eroding Nvidia’s pricing power too, as detailed next.

Nvidia: More Threatened By Google Than Chip Peers

The latest development in the AI ecosystem has led me to form the view is Nvidia is more likely to be threatened by hyperscalers like Google (and Amazon (AMZN), to a secondary degree) than by chip peers like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). In particular, Google has recently released its new Gemini 3 model (shortly after my last NVDA analysis). The market responses on the model, both from institutional analysts and also users, have been quite positive. Gemini 3 marks a hardware milestone in my view for Google custom silicon effort. The model is trained on Google’s own AI chips known as TPUs (Tensor Processing Units). After several generations, these chips have evolved sufficiently to be a meaningful threat to Nvidia's GPUs for AI applications, in my view. The Gemini 3 model was trained with a combination of the 5th and 6th generations(v5e and the v6). I won’t be surprised to see the next generation (TPUv7) to find wide adoption soon too. As a reflection, key customers like Meta Platforms (META) and Anthropic was reported to be in talks with Google to buy these TPUs for their AI applications.

Based on the specifications available (see an example below), my assessment is that the total cost of ownership (including upfront purchasing cost and also ongoing power cost) for Google’s TPUv7 would be about 40% lower than Nvidia’s GB200 chips. Besides Google, other hyperscalers like Amazon have also made considerable progress in recent years with products specifically targeting machine learning (ML, such as AMZN’s Trainium chips) and high-performance storage products. With the rise of these products, I don’t expect Nvidia to be the only viable option for large-scale AI inference, which could both impact its sales growth and also its profit margins.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

These above uncertainties are also reflected in the stock’s recent technical trading patterns. As seen in the chart below, the share price has been oscillating around its 20-day moving average (MA) since August, which has flattened itself and lost its upward momentum. More recently, since early November, the chart has been forming a downward wedging pattern between a peak of ~$210 and a support near $170. Furthermore, the bottom panel highlights a 14-day Relative Strength Index (RSI) of 52, a quite neutral reflection of a close balance between buying and selling pressure.

The last technical signal I want to draw your attention to is the price-volume bar highlighted by the blue box. This is the trading window that has attracted the large trading volumes in the past 6 months. NVDA’s current stock price is square within this range of around $175 to $185. I anticipate it to take a while for the large number of investors participating in this range to be replaced by, or converted to, with another group of investors with a renewed bullish view, especially amid the fundamental uncertainties mentioned above.

Finally, in terms of valuation, the stock is also on the expensive side, both in absolute terms and relative to close peers. As an example, compared to the mag 7 group (see the next table), NVDA’s current FWD P/E of 39.17x is only second to TSLA.

To close, this NVDA follow-up was mainly motivated by three new developments since my last writing: its China market, internal silicon developments from hyperscalers, and also the latest technical trading indicators. Combined with elevated valuation risks, these catalysts suggest neutral outlook to me and I see large odds for the stocks prices to be range bound in 2026.

data.title: Nebius: A Gift That Will Keep On Giving
data.description:
This summer we called for a public Buy rating on Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) in the mid-$40s. There was a major run in shares the next few months. More recently, there has been corrective behavior, and the further the shares dipped under $100, the more we got behind it once again. Tech has rebounded some, and NBIS is off recent lows.

On the surface, the stock looks expensive, but when you factor in the ongoing growth, major company AI-related spending, and the investments being made to build out the AI infrastructure, NBIS shares are attractive in the long run. With the holidays upon us, consider stuffing your stocking with a few shares of NBIS on the big dips. It will be a gift that keeps on giving. Does this mean it goes straight up? Absolutely not. Will shares be higher one year from now? Barring a market meltdown, the answer is yes. Folks, we want to be clear. We maintain the position that Nebius is a long-term winner.

Growth driven by explosive revenue growth and increased ARR

The big-time growth is being driven by big-time expansion in revenue, as well as significant expansion in recurring revenue (aka its ARR). As we move into 2026, we are on the watch for the making of new deals and bringing in new customers. Given the huge investments being made to expand the company and meet customer needs, the company has been burning through a lot of cash. This burning of cash the last handful of quarters is in direct relation to the massive focus on building out AI infrastructure. It reflects the massive capex spending big players are making. And a number of companies are beneficiaries, including Nebius.

Of course, this catalyst is also a risk. If capex spending comes down meaningfully on AI spend, Nebius and other competitors will feel the pinch. But for now, it's bulls on parade. While losing money to expand rapidly and meet customer needs, Nebius is in a great position to help meet the needs for large-scale GPUs and cloud platforms. Nebius has, of course, responded to the demand by raising capital to fund ongoing projects. We know it has some major deals out there with big Mag-7 names, like Meta (META). Make no mistake, the hypergrowth is set to continue in 2026.

Expanding key metrics cannot be ignored

When the company reported Q3 earnings, revenue surged to $146.1 million, a massive increase from the $105 million in the sequential Q2 alone. This was also up from $55 million in Q1 of 2026, an eye-popping increase of 355% year-over-year. While that immense pace of revenue growth cannot last due to the law of large numbers, the growth in absolute dollars on the top line is set to continue. It all stems from spending cash on its investments in the core AI infrastructure business. The all-important ARR figure? It had been ramping toward a key $1 billion milestone this year. By the end of next year, that ARR is set to be $7-$9 billion with contracted power to hit 2.5GW. Yes, heavy losses are to continue, but margin power and EBITDA are improving significantly.

So, as you can expect, as revenues surge, so will the cost of revenue. As we saw recently, those costs ballooned $42.9 million from $9.9 million last year and were up $12.8 million from the sequential quarter. All cost lines are rising, which should not be a surprise. You have to spend money to make money, as they say. While selling expenses, depreciation, and R&D costs have all surged, as of now, as a percentage of revenues, all of these expenses are actually lower. As the company builds out to reach its target $7-$9 billion in ARR by this time next year, expect operating expenses to increase dramatically.

Like many others in this space, share-based compensation is also a line to watch. It is not just an expense but is dilutive. This compensation keeps talent in-house and gives them "skin in the game." It is not going away any time soon.

Losing money, for how long?

Now, we are out here suggesting you stuff your stockings with some shares. Should you load up all at once? No, but on the recent pullback we saw in tech, that was a good time to add. Much like a family starts its holiday shopping well in advance, so should you, picking up sales when they arise. We just had one. We may or may not have a Santa rally this week and next, but pick your posts. We fully acknowledge that investing here is investing in the future. And that is a bet. As mentioned, if AI spending comes down in the future, Nebius and competitors will take a hit. As it stands now, despite the huge revenue growth, as expenses ramp up for the company, Nebius still loses big money. The question is how long will that continue? At least a few more quarters to be sure.

Each time capital is raised, it is quickly spent on building out capacity and retaining talent. That comes too with being diluted. That is a fair, bearish point. But EBITDA is trending in the right direction. Adjusted EBITDA was still a loss in Q3, but just $5.2 million, much better than the $45.9 million last year. However, on the bottom line as adjusted, losses are mounting. Year-over-year losses ballooned 153% to $100.4 million compared to last year. Sequentially, about another additional $10 million was lost. However, as more and more deals are put into place, capacity expansion expenses normalize some, revenues come in from the partnerships, and revenue growth continues with improving margins, earnings positivity, and positive cash flow are in the cards. Will that be in 2026? Unlikely. However, 2027 could be where the tide turns.

Early estimates suggest another year of losses in 2026, but 2027, for now, looks to swing positive. We, of course, need to get updates and guidance from the company to better understand progress on meeting revenue targets, get a feel for the trend in expense growth, and understand cash burn, but the early outlook now is for inflection in the bottom line in 2027.

Do earnings matter? Of course, yes. But at this stage of the game, it is still about ramping revenue and capacity. As these are achieved, the Street will then shift to caring more about the bottom line. For now, shares look expensive, but considering where the company can potentially be in 2-3 years, the big-time pullbacks are a bargain to consider here. It takes time before the massive spending can normalize. So, expect ongoing net losses for the next year, but watch for inflection. We think having some exposure to this hypergrowth on weakness is worth the investment. Take advantage of the holiday sale on shares.

data.title: U.S. GDP Delivers Another Christmas Surprise While Canadian Economy Contracts - Market Reactions
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Santa came with another positive surprise for the US, but despite the snow, there wasn't any Christmas miracle for Canada.

The US GDP release was indeed a strong hit, coming in at an annualized 4.3% (with the price index for Q3 coming in at 3.7% vs. 2.7% expected).

Stellar numbers on the surface, but the strong data may raise a few questions.

A significant portion of this growth could be driven by a sharp decrease in imports, which mathematically boosts the headline GDP figure even if domestic demand isn't accelerating at the same pace.

It’s a classic accounting quirk that could mask underlying softness - or at least, make the economy look hotter than the labor market suggests.

Industrial production offered a glimmer of stabilization, confirming your figures with a -0.1% read for October and a +0.2% rebound in November, still totalling for a two-month (slight) increase.

We will likely have to wait for the final yearly publications to see if this is a genuine rise.

Yet, the S&P 500 doesn't seem to care, shaking off an open gap-down but overall seesawing action for stocks.

Canada's cutting cycle - slashing rates from 5.50% to 2.25% since mid 2024 -hasn't yet sparked a revival, confirmed by the disappointing -0.3% GDP print for October.

But as GDP is a lagging data and employment showed decent upside, the upbeat data should reflect in the GDP only later next year.

Let's explore reactions to the data with our market overview.

Volatility should compress as we approach the rest of the day and the end of the year.

Of course, keep an eye on any geopolitical disruptions that could be the only real factor for movement, particularly if nobody is there to absorb liquidity shocks. But as long as nothing happens, expect a slow, sideways grind for the next few weeks.

Safe trades, and merry Christmas!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia Is Not A Bubble, Here's Why
data.description:
As I said several times before, the first time I invested in Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) was in late 2022. Since then, NVDA has been one of my favorite businesses to follow and continue investing. Yes, I invested many times since then, even this year, so I can state right from the get-go that I don't consider NVDA overvalued.

NVDA's performance in 2025 was very strong and I believe it proved its capabilities to navigate around geopolitical shifts and tensions (I've explained that in detail in this article, so feel free to take a look if you'd like). Since the tariff-related dip, NVDA's stock price has increased by over 88% - I can see it's quite stabilized at current levels ranging from $170 to $200 for a couple of months now.

Since the last time I covered NVDA, we received several important insights from both the Company and other market participants. I've observed increasing concerns about the "AI bubble" and "Nvidia's overvaluation." For instance, Reuters reported yesterday that many investors are betting on China instead of the U.S. in terms of AI technology - all of that is naturally connected to the fear of a "bubble":

Global investors turn to Chinese AI as Wall Street fears bubble.

(...)

Global investors are increasing their wagers on Chinese artificial intelligence companies, betting on the next DeepSeek and seeking to diversify, with concerns growing about a speculative bubble in the sector on Wall Street.

So... I decided to prepare a follow-up coverage to show these insights I consider some of the key factors of NVDA's further growth and sector domination. But first things first - I'd like to address the so-called "AI bubble" first, so that Nvidia's investors (including me) can rest assured.

AI is not a bubble, and neither is Nvidia - here's why

I understand investors are concerned about the risk of forming a bubble. It's a healthy approach, we should all be skeptical - that's how we remain reasonable with our investing decisions. I have no doubts that there are investors who genuinely fear that the technological market reached a bubble - I often see comparisons to a dot-com bubble:

The immense amount of investment being poured into AI is an undisputed fact, companies and investors are piling billions into the ecosystem, driving valuations to unseen heights. Comparisons between today’s artificial intelligence boom and the dot-com bubble of the late 1990s and early 2000s are becoming increasingly common. Some commentators even claim the US economy is hinging on this bubble and is bound to crash when it pops. In both cases, we see the rise of a transformative technology, a rapid surge in company valuations, and soaring expectations.

But... there's one crucial thing such commentators are missing - earnings potential. We can easily analyze it through valuation multiples. Why do they matter? While valuation is an important aspect of making investment decisions, the key is to review valuation relative to income potential. In simpler words - I always try to answer the following question: how many dollars do I have to pay for one dollar of earnings that a given business generates?

The chart above shows why investors are afraid that Nvidia is just another bubble as they compare the dynamic valuation growth to the symbol of a dot-com bubble - Cisco (CSCO). Now, NVDA's valuation is even higher than presented in the chart. However, the picture looks differently when we account for the income potential via reviewing forward-looking P/E multiple, doesn't it?

At the top of the bubble Cisco climbed as high as ~100x 2-year forward-looking P/E ratio - I think that if the comparison between the current market levels ("AI bubble") and the dot-com bubble was solid, multiples of the most relevant players should be at least comparable. Is that the case?

I wanted to show you a summary of key hardware providers that facilitate the ongoing AI revolution (including NVDA) - the chart above serves this purpose. As you can see, none of these businesses trades at nearly 100x multiples and on top of that, it's not like NVDA's multiples are the highest. There's even a case to be made that some of them are heavily undervalued - I think Micron is a perfect example of that situation, as I explained in my latest coverage (here's the link).

I think the same applies to businesses playing at the front-line of the AI revolution (check the chart above) - clients of businesses I mentioned earlier that invest heavily in data center infrastructure to facilitate their cloud and computing capabilities.

My verdict? Nvidia is not a bubble, it's not overvalued, and I still see its significant potential to not only grow into its valuation further but to keep increasing it as it reaches next milestones. In fact... as I mentioned earlier, I have a few insights to share with you.

Three updates making the bull case stronger

#1 Nvidia pushes its open-source AI offering further

Is Nvidia a hardware or a software business? Naturally, it's mainly recognized as a hardware business, but I like to keep in mind that NVDA's software (mainly CUDA) is as crucial as every other aspect of its business. As Reuters mentioned:

Its proprietary CUDA software, a standard among most developers, is a major selling point for its chips, making software key to maintaining its dominance in the AI industry.

Also, it offers a handful of open-source models that other businesses can use to build their own products - it keeps developing the range of these models with new addition announced last week (Nemotron) and some announced to be released in 2026:

Nvidia is primarily known for providing chips that firms such as OpenAI use to train their closed-source models and charge money for them. But it also offers a slew of its own models for everything from physics simulations to self-driving vehicles as open-source software that can be used by researchers or by other companies, with firms such as Palantir Technologies weaving Nvidia's model into their products.

Nvidia on Monday revealed the third generation of its "Nemotron" large-language models aimed at writing, coding and other tasks. The smallest of the models, called Nemotron 3 Nano, was being released Monday, with two other, larger versions coming in the first half of 2026.

Some of you probably know this already - I'm an M&A advisor, so I always like to see some deals, as they are especially interesting to me given my day-to-day occupation. That's why Nvidia surprised me positively with SchedMD acquisition that further strengthens its open-source offer:

NVIDIA today announced it has acquired SchedMD — the leading developer of Slurm, an open-source workload management system for high-performance computing (HPC) and AI — to help strengthen the open-source software ecosystem and drive AI innovation for researchers, developers and enterprises.

(...)

Slurm, which is supported on the latest NVIDIA hardware, is also part of the critical infrastructure needed for generative AI, used by foundation model developers and AI builders to manage model training and inference needs.

#2 The Chinese market will reopen and strengthen soon

Ach, the export to China... it was quite a ride wasn't it? A lot happened on that front in 2025, but I was happy to read just yesterday that Nvidia wants to start shipping its H200 chips to China in just two months from now (around the half of February 2026). Reuters reported that:

The U.S. chipmaker plans to fulfil initial orders from existing stock, with shipments expected to total 5,000 to 10,000 chip modules - equivalent to about 40,000 to 80,000 H200 AI chips, the first and second sources said.

How's the demand? I think it'll be strong. In fact, it's expected to be strong enough to incline Nvidia to increase its output of H200 (while stating clearly that entering China again won't impact the supply to U.S. clients):

Nvidia (NVDA.O), opens new tab has told Chinese clients it is evaluating adding production capacity for its powerful H200 AI chips after orders exceeded its current output level, according to two sources briefed on the matter.

The move comes after U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday the U.S. government would allow Nvidia to export H200 processors, its second-fastest AI chips, to China and collect a 25% fee on such sales.

(...)

"We are managing our supply chain to ensure that licensed sales of the H200 to authorized customers in China will have no impact on our ability to supply customers in the United States," an Nvidia spokesperson said in a statement to Reuters after the story was published.

Major Chinese companies including Alibaba (9988.HK), opens new tab and ByteDance have already reached out to Nvidia this week about purchasing the H200 and are keen to place large orders, Reuters reported on Wednesday.

#3 Stellar product roadmap ahead with Rubin going to market in 2026

Nvidia's Executive VP & CFO (Colette Kress) has recently presented at UBS Global Technology and AI Conference 2025 - I was happy to read the transcript of that conference, as Colette Kress gave us some solid updates on NVDA's market outlook and product progress. What do I have in mind?

Firstly, Nvidia is looking at a $3T to $4T opportunity in the broad data center market by the end of 2030 (I understand it's attributable to several factors, incl. changes of chips from previous architectures to new ones, expanding computing demand, as well as progressing transition from CPUs to GPUs).

So when we think about our outlook of by the end of the decade, $3 trillion to $4 trillion worth of AI or just total data center infrastructure (...)

How will NVDA capture as much of the market as it can? Just take a look at the product roadmap it has ahead of itself - after a smooth and successful introduction of Blackwell Nvidia is working hard on the Rubin architecture with Feynman expected for 2028. Vera Rubin should go to market in the second half of 2026:

We're very pleased both with what occurred with Ultra. People come in and say, it was seamless, and that's what we wanted. So a seamless transition, very, very helpful for many in the new models that they were creating. And I think you're going to see an X factor increase also with performance as we think about Vera Rubin. So it's right around the corner for the second half of next year. We're very excited for it.

My conclusion? Nvidia is a "Strong Buy"

I established a few things in this article. I think the most important aspect is showing the difference between the dot-com bubble and the current stage of the market - Nvidia is not a bubble in my opinion and its valuation multiples (as well as multiples of other key market players) confirm that. While NVDA is not "cheap," buying at ~39x forward-looking earnings can still prove profitable for investors. Please keep in mind that Nvidia traded at even higher levels throughout the last couple of years and buying it worked pretty well. How's that possible? Growth. Nvidia is one of the most capable businesses in terms of growing into its valuation.

Regarding the insights I shared, I believe each of them supports Nvidia's growth story. Promising acquisitions, expanding open-source offering, strong progress on new architectures, and getting the Chinese market up and running again - I see these news as highly positive and believe they make the bull case even stronger (not to mention high double-digit revenue growth recording each quarter).

Despite a stabilized stock price level recorded during the last couple of months, I think investors can still expect high double-digit total returns as Nvidia keeps growing its scale and bottom-line, as well as cementing the leadership position. That's why NVDA remains a Strong Buy to me.
